clean_story:
Written words. Spoken rhythms.
Arabic’s name comes from ʿarabiyyah—the language of the Arabs—but it’s more like a living archive: 1,500 years old, yet still shaping news, poetry, and street signs from Casablanca to Baghdad.

Start with the alphabet: 28 letters, each with 2–4 shapes depending on where they sit in a word. Think of them as chameleons—ب (b) looks like a bowl with a dot (بـ ـبـ ـب) but stretches or shrinks to fit its neighbors. Vowels hide as tiny marks above or below, like secret handshakes for pronunciation. Skip them in newspapers; keep them in Quranic verses.

Dialects split Arabic like rivers. A "hello" in Cairo ("Ahlan") becomes "Marhaba" in Dubai, "Salam" in Damascus. Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) is the formal bridge—used in books and speeches—but no one chats in it. It’s like writing in Shakespearean English while texting in slang.

The root system is the skeleton: three consonants (e.g., k-t-b) spin into words like kitāb (book), maktab (desk), kātib (writer). Memorize 200 roots, and suddenly 10,000 words make sense.

Listen to Fairuz’s songs or watch Bab al-Hara—your ear will catch the musical ayn (ع), a guttural hum like a held breath. Mistake it for ghayn (غ), and "love" (ḥubb) turns into "war" (ḥarb).

Three related subjects are root-based languages, diglossia in linguistics, and calligraphy as code.
